text: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam v. State of Andhra Pradesh, Supreme Court of India, decided 28/07/2000 (AIR 2000 SC 2511; (2000) 7 SCC 72). Accident on 17.12.1993 involving an Andhra Pradesh Road Transport Corporation bus: passenger Agamma fell while boarding as the bus moved forward; rear wheel ran over her causing death; driver convicted under Section 304A IPC (three months imprisonment) by trial court, affirmed by Sessions Court and High Court in revision. Core issue: whether negligent/rash driving can be inferred or presumed from the mere fact of a passenger falling while boarding, and applicability of res ipsa loquitur in criminal negligence. Supreme Court held no automatic presumption of negligence in motor accidents; res ipsa loquitur is only a rule of evidence shifting onus when circumstances show accident would not occur without negligence and instrumentality was under defendant’s control. Since no witness (including conductor) stated driver moved before signal and evidence was scanty, conviction under Section 304A IPC was unsustainable; appeal allowed and driver acquitted.

metadata.case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh

text: On 17.12.1993 the appellant, a driver of an Andhra Pradesh Road Transport Corporation bus, moved the bus forward when a passenger, Agamma, was boarding it enroute. Agamma fell out of the vehicle and the rear wheel ran over her. She died of the injuries sustained. The driver was convicted by the trial court under Section 304A IPC, and the conviction was affirmed by the Sessions Court and the High Court in revision, leading to the appeal to the Supreme Court.

metadata.case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh

text: The Court held that "It is a wrong proposition that for any motor accident negligence of the driver should be presumed" and that "Merely because a passenger fell down from the bus while boarding the bus no presumption of negligence can be drawn against the driver of the bus." It explained that the principle of "res ipsa loquitor is only a rule of evidence" and applies only when the nature of the accident and circumstances reasonably indicate the accident would not have occurred "in the absence of negligence" and the injuring thing was under the control of the alleged wrongdoer. On the proved facts, "no witness has said, including the conductor, that the driver moved the vehicle before getting signal to move forward" and the "evidence in this case is too scanty to fasten him with criminal negligence." As "Such further evidence is lacking in this case," the Court concluded it was "disabled from concluding that the victim fell down only because of the negligent driving of the bus," rendering the conviction under Section 304A IPC "unsustainable" and requiring acquittal.

metadata.case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh

text: The appeal was allowed; the conviction and sentence under Section 304A IPC were set aside and the appellant was acquitted.

metadata.case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh

text: The appellant challenged the conclusion that he was guilty of negligent driving merely because the passenger fell while boarding. He contended that there was insufficient evidence that he started the bus suddenly before she could board or that he moved the bus without a conductor’s signal, and therefore criminal negligence could not be presumed on the scant evidence.

ALLCRILR 763, 2000 (2) ANDHLT(CRI) 158 SC, (2000) 5 BOM CR 163
Bench: R.P.Sethi, K.T.Thomas
PETITIONER:
MOHAMMED AYNUDDIN @ MIYAM
Vs.
RESPONDENT:
STATE OF ANDHRA PRADESH
DATE OF JUDGMENT: 28/07/2000
BENCH:
R.P.Sethi, K.T.Thomas
JUDGMENT:
THOMAS, J.
Leave granted.
A passenger, while boarding a bus, fell down therefrom as the vehicle moved forward. The driver of
the bus was held guilty of culpable negligence in that episode. He now stands convicted under
Section 304A of Indian Penal Code and was sentenced to imprisonment for three months. All the
three courts, the trial court, the Sessions Court and the High Court in revision - took the same stand.
Hence this appeal. The finding of facts cannot be disturbed now. The only question which survives
 1

Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh on 28 July, 2000
for decision is whether on such facts a conclusion that the appellant is guilty of negligent driving
must necessarily follow. The facts which the courts found to have been established in the case are
these:
On 17.12.1993 the appellant was driving a bus of the Andhra Pradesh Road Transport
Corporation. A passenger by name Agamma boarded the bus enroute at some point.
When the bus moved forward she fell out of the vehicle and its rear wheel ran over
her. She died of the injuries sustained in that accident.
T

metadata.case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh

text:
ndhra Pradesh Road Transport
Corporation. A passenger by name Agamma boarded the bus enroute at some point.
When the bus moved forward she fell out of the vehicle and its rear wheel ran over
her. She died of the injuries sustained in that accident.
The conductor of the bus was examined as PW3. He did not say how the accident
happened. However, he admitted that while the bus was in motion he heard a sound
of accident and the bus was then stopped. The only witness who spoke about the
occurrence was PW4. What that witness has deposed in the examination-in-chief is
the following:
"Agamma was boarding the bus and the bus was moved; and she fell down beneath
the bus and died on the spot; the bus stopped at some distance. I saw the driver of the
bus at that time.
What is the culpable negligence on the part of the bus driver in the above accident? A
passenger might fall down from a moving vehicle due to one of the following causes:
It could be accidental; it could be due to the negligence of the passenger himself; it
could be due to the negligent taking off of the bus by the driver. However, to fasten
the liability with the driver for negligent driving in such a situation there should be
the evidence that he moved the bus suddenly before the passenger could get into the
vehicle or that the driver moved the vehicle even before getting any signal from the
rear side.
A driver who moves the bus forward can be expected to keep his eyes ahead and
possibly on the sides also. A driver can take the reverse motion when that driver
assures himself that the vehicle can safely be taken backward.
It is a wrong proposition that for any motor accident negligence of the driver should
be presumed. An accident of such a nature as would prima facie show that it cannot
be accounted to anything other than the negligence of the driver of the vehicle may
create a presumption and in such a case the driver has to explain how the accident
happened without negligence on his part. Merely because a passenger fell down from
the bus while boarding the bus no presumption of negligence can be drawn against
the driver of the bus.
The principle of res ipsa loquitor is only a rule of evidence to determine

metadata.case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh

text:
hout negligence on his part. Merely because a passenger fell down from
the bus while boarding the bus no presumption of negligence can be drawn against
the driver of the bus.
The principle of res ipsa loquitor is only a rule of evidence to determine the onus of
proof in actions relating to negligence. The said principle has application only when
the nature of the accident and the attending circumstances would reasonably lead to
the belief that in the absence of negligence the accident would not have occurred and
 2

Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh on 28 July, 2000
that the thing which caused injury is shown to have been under the management and
control of the alleged wrong doer.
A rash act is primarily an over hasty act. It is opposed to a deliberate act. Still a rash
act can be a deliberate act in the sense that it was done without due care and caution.
Culpable rashness lies in running the risk of doing an act with recklessness and with
indifference as to the consequences. Criminal negligence is the failure to exercise
duty with reasonable and proper care and precaution guarding against injury to the
public generally or to any individual in particular. It is the imperative duty of the
driver of a vehicle to adopt such reasonable and proper care and precaution.
In the present case the possible explanation of the driver is that he was unaware of
even the possibility of the accident which happened. It could be so. When he moved
the vehicle forward his focus normally would have been towards what was ahead of
the vehicle. He is not expected to move the vehicle forward when passengers are in
the process of boarding the vehicle. But when he gets a signal from the conductor that
the bus can proceed he is expected to start moving the vehicle. Here no witness has
said, including the conductor, that the driver moved the vehicle before getting signal
to move forward. The evidence in this case is too scanty to fasten him with criminal
negligence. Some further evidence is indispensably needed to presume that the
passenger fell down due to the negligence of the driver of the bus. Such further
evidence is lacking in this case. Therefore, the court is disa

metadata.case_name: Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State Of Andhra Pradesh

text:
to fasten him with criminal
negligence. Some further evidence is indispensably needed to presume that the
passenger fell down due to the negligence of the driver of the bus. Such further
evidence is lacking in this case. Therefore, the court is disabled from concluding that
the victim fell down only because of the negligent driving of the bus. The corollary
thereof is that the conviction of the appellant of the offence is unsustainable. In the
result, we allow this appeal and set aside the conviction and sentence and he is
acquitted.
